attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by his son with progressive confusion and altered mental state for three days. He has not eaten since yesterday. He has an eight-month history of dry cough and 10 kg weight loss. He has a 42-year smoking history but no other significant medical history. He takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He is difficult to rouse and does not respond to commands, groaning only to painful stimuli. His mucous membranes are moist. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

His investigations reveal:

  • Sodium = 124 mmol/L (135-145)
  • Potassium = 4.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.2)
  • Chloride = 92 mmol/L (95-110)
  • Bicarbonate = 24 mmol/L (22-28)
  • Calcium = 2.32 mmol/L (2.10-2.60)
  • Blood glucose = 4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.4)
  • Serum osmolality = 220 mmol/kg (275-295)
  • Urine osmolality = 450 mmol/kg (50-1,200)

Arterial blood gas analysis shows:

  • pH = 7.40 (7.35-7.45)
  • pCO2 = 40 mmHg (34-45)
  • pO2 = 89 mmHg (80-100)

A chest X-ray shows a mass in the left hilar region.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of small cell lung cancer (chronic cough, weight loss, smoking history, hilar mass) complicated by syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH). The diagnosis of SIADH is supported by euvolaemic hyponatraemia with low serum osmolality and inappropriately high urine osmolality.

Hypertonic saline is the most appropriate management given the presence of severe neurological symptoms (altered mental state). While fluid restriction alone might be appropriate for asymptomatic hyponatraemia, the presence of CNS symptoms necessitates more aggressive correction. The rate of correction should not exceed 4-8 mmol/L per day to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome, particularly in chronic cases.

Fluid restriction alone would be appropriate for asymptomatic hyponatraemia with sodium levels above 120 mmol/L, but is insufficient given this patient's neurological symptoms.

5% dextrose would be inappropriate as it is hypotonic and would worsen the hyponatraemia by further diluting serum sodium. It is more commonly used in treating hypernatraemia.

Normal saline would be ineffective as its osmolality is similar to that of the patient's serum. It may actually worsen hyponatraemia by increasing total body water without adequately increasing sodium concentration.

Intravenous desmopressin is an antidiuretic hormone analogue that would exacerbate the SIADH by promoting further water retention. It is used in diabetes insipidus, where ADH is deficient.
learningPoints: Hypertonic saline is indicated for hyponatraemia with neurological symptoms, regardless of sodium level.
Small cell lung cancer commonly presents with paraneoplastic SIADH causing euvolaemic hyponatraemia.
The rate of sodium correction should not exceed 4-8 mmol/L per day to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.
references: Electrolyte abnormalities [published 2014 Mar]. In: eTG complete [digital]. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; 2021 Mar. <https://www.tg.org.au>
